name: Statistics for Social Sciences
description: Applied statistics for understanding social phenomena — data collection, survey design, descriptive and inferential statistics, correlation, regression, and critically reading quantitative social research.

modules.title: Introduction: Why Statistics Matter in Social Science
modules.titleZh: 导论：为什么统计学对社会科学重要
modules.objectives: Explain how data drives policy, research, and public debate; identify real-world applications of social statistics.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1: Sampling and Data
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #1 — What Is Statistics?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Identifying Population and Sample
modules.assignment: Find one news article that uses statistics. Identify the claim being made, the data source cited, and evaluate whether the statistic is used accurately or misleadingly.

modules.title: Types of Data & Measurement
modules.objectives: Distinguish between nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio levels of measurement; explain operationalization of social concepts.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1.1: Definitions and Key Terms
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Categorical and Quantitative Data
modules.assignment: Choose a social concept (e.g., poverty, education level, political affiliation). Write a paragraph operationalizing it at each of the four measurement levels, explaining what you gain or lose at each level.

modules.title: Survey Design & Sampling
modules.objectives: Construct well-designed survey questions; compare sampling methods; identify sources of bias and their effects on results.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1.2: Data, Sampling, and Variation
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Sampling Methods and Avoiding Bias
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #4 — Bias in Surveys and Studies
modules.assignment: Design a 5-question survey on a social issue of your choice. For each question, identify potential bias and revise it. Specify the sampling method you would use and explain why.

modules.title: Describing Data: Frequencies & Distributions
modules.objectives: Construct frequency tables and histograms; interpret skewness and kurtosis in social data distributions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 2: Descriptive Statistics
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Creating and Interpreting Histograms
modules.assignment: Using publicly available data (e.g., Census income data), create a frequency table and histogram. Describe the shape of the distribution and what it tells you about inequality.

modules.title: Measures of Center & Spread
modules.titleZh: 集中趋势与离散趋势的度量
modules.objectives: Calculate and interpret mean, median, mode, variance, standard deviation, and IQR; choose the appropriate measure for different data types.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 2.3–2.7: Measures of Center and Spread
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Mean, Median, and Mode
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #3 — Measures of Spread
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Calculating Variance and Standard Deviation
modules.assignment: Given a dataset of household incomes in a neighborhood, calculate mean, median, and standard deviation. Explain why the median is often preferred over the mean for income data.

modules.title: Probability & Risk in Social Context
modules.titleZh: 社会情境中的概率与风险
modules.objectives: Apply basic probability rules; calculate and interpret relative risk and odds ratios; recognize the base rate fallacy in social research.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 3: Probability Topics
modules.assignment: A news headline states: 'People who drink coffee are 50% more likely to develop anxiety.' Evaluate this claim. Compute a hypothetical relative risk and odds ratio using made-up but realistic numbers, and explain the base rate fallacy in this context.

modules.title: The Normal Distribution & Z-Scores
modules.objectives: Describe properties of the normal distribution; convert raw scores to z-scores; use z-scores to find percentiles and probabilities.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 6: The Normal Distribution
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #18 — Z-Scores and Percentiles
modules.assignment: A standardized test has a mean of 500 and SD of 100. Calculate z-scores for scores of 350, 500, and 650. Determine the percentile for each and explain what this tells us about the test-takers.

modules.title: Correlation
modules.objectives: Construct and interpret scatterplots; calculate and interpret Pearson r and Spearman rho; explain why correlation does not imply causation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 12: Linear Regression and Correlation
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #10 — Correlation Doesn't Equal Causation
modules.assignment: Find or generate a dataset with two social variables (e.g., education years and income). Create a scatterplot, estimate the direction and strength of correlation, and write a paragraph explaining what conclusions you can and cannot draw.

modules.title: Simple Linear Regression
modules.objectives: Interpret the regression equation, slope, and intercept; use regression to make predictions; evaluate model fit using R².
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 12.3: The Regression Equation
modules.assignment: Using a social dataset of your choice, fit a simple linear regression. Write the equation, interpret the slope in plain language, and explain what R² tells you about how well education predicts income (or your chosen variables).

modules.title: Multiple Regression Overview
modules.objectives: Explain how adding predictors changes a regression model; interpret coefficients while controlling for other variables; identify confounders.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 13: Linear Regression and Correlation (Extended)
modules.assignment: A researcher regresses income on education alone (r² = 0.25), then adds age and gender to get r² = 0.48. Explain in 200 words what changed and why controlling for confounders matters in social research.

modules.title: Confidence Intervals in Practice
modules.objectives: Construct and interpret confidence intervals; explain margin of error in polling; connect CI width to sample size and confidence level.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 8: Confidence Intervals
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Confidence Intervals and Margin of Error
modules.assignment: A political poll reports 52% support for a candidate with a ±3% margin of error at 95% confidence. Explain what this means, what it does not mean, and how you would correctly report this finding to a general audience.

modules.title: Hypothesis Testing in Social Research
modules.titleZh: 社会研究中的假设检验
modules.objectives: Conduct and interpret t-tests and chi-square tests; explain p-values and statistical significance; discuss the replication crisis.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 9: Hypothesis Testing with One Sample
modules.assignment: A study finds that students in small classes score 5 points higher on average than those in large classes (p = 0.03). Write a 250-word interpretation: What does p = 0.03 mean? Is this result practically significant? What are the risks of relying only on p-values?

modules.title: Critically Reading Research
modules.objectives: Evaluate study design quality; spot misleading statistics and data visualizations; assess how media reports quantitative findings.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Introductory Statistics — Ch 1.3: Frequency and Data Presentation
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Experiment vs. Observational Study
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Statistics #15 — Errors and How to Avoid Them
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Observational Studies vs. Experiments
modules.assignment: Find a news article reporting a scientific or social study. Evaluate: (1) Was it experimental or observational? (2) Is causation claimed from correlation? (3) Is the sample representative? (4) Is the graph (if any) misleading? Write a 300-word critique.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best reason social scientists use statistics?
options: To make research sound more scientific
To systematically describe patterns and test hypotheses about social behavior
questions.answer: To systematically describe patterns and test hypotheses about social behavior
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: To systematically describe patterns and test hypotheses about social behavior.

questions.question: A researcher measures 'political ideology' on a scale of 1 (far left) to 7 (far right). This is best classified as:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Ordinal.

questions.question: Which level of measurement has a meaningful true zero?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Ratio.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Operationalization refers to:" is: Defining how an abstract concept will be measured in practice.
questions.explanation: True. Defining how an abstract concept will be measured in practice is correct.

questions.question: In random sampling, every member of the population:
options: Has an equal chance of being selected
questions.answer: Has an equal chance of being selected
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Has an equal chance of being selected.

questions.question: A histogram with its tail extending to the right is described as:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Positively skewed.

questions.question: Which measure of center is most affected by extreme outliers?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Mean.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The interquartile range (IQR) is defined as:" is: The range between the minimum and maximum.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Q3 minus Q1.

questions.question: If a disease affects 2 out of 1000 people in a general population, and 4 out of 1000 smokers, the relative risk for smokers is:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 2.0.

questions.question: The base rate fallacy occurs when:
options: A researcher uses too large a sample
A test has a very high false positive rate
People ignore the underlying frequency of an event when evaluating probability
questions.answer: People ignore the underlying frequency of an event when evaluating probability
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: People ignore the underlying frequency of an event when evaluating probability.

questions.question: In a normal distribution, approximately what percentage of data falls within one standard deviation of the mean?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 68%.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A z-score of -1.5 indicates that an observation is:" is: 1.5 standard deviations below the mean.
questions.explanation: True. 1.5 standard deviations below the mean is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following survey questions is most clearly biased?
options: Do you support the irresponsible decision to cut school funding?
What is your annual household income?
How many hours per week do you work?
questions.answer: Do you support the irresponsible decision to cut school funding?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Do you support the irresponsible decision to cut school funding?.

questions.question: Stratified sampling involves:
options: Selecting every 10th person on a list
Dividing the population into subgroups and sampling from each
questions.answer: Dividing the population into subgroups and sampling from each
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Dividing the population into subgroups and sampling from each.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a population parameter and a sample statistic. Give one example of each in the context of social research on unemployment rates.
questions.answer: A population parameter is a numerical value describing the entire population (e.g., the true national unemployment rate). A sample statistic is a value computed from a subset (e.g., the unemployment rate found in a survey of 2,000 workers, used to estimate the national rate).
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A population parameter is a numerical value describing the entire population (e.g., the true national unemployment rate). A sample statistic is a value computed from a subset (e.g., the unemployment rate found in a survey of 2,000 workers, used to estimate the national rate)..

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is a ratio-level variable" is: Religion (Catholic, Protestant, Other).
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Number of years of education.

questions.question: A frequency table showing the proportion of each category is called a:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Relative frequency table.

questions.question: Which statistic is most appropriate for describing the center of a strongly skewed income distribution?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Median.

questions.question: A Pearson correlation of r = -0.82 indicates:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A strong negative relationship.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Spearman's rho is preferred over Pearson r when:" is: Data are ordinal or not normally distributed.
questions.explanation: True. Data are ordinal or not normally distributed is correct.

questions.question: In a regression equation ŷ = 2.5x + 10, the slope of 2.5 means:
options: The predicted value of y when x = 0 is 2.5
For each one-unit increase in x, y is predicted to increase by 2.5
There is a 2.5% chance of the relationship being real
questions.answer: For each one-unit increase in x, y is predicted to increase by 2.5
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: For each one-unit increase in x, y is predicted to increase by 2.5.

questions.question: R² = 0.36 in a regression model means:
options: 36% of the variance in the outcome is explained by the predictor(s)
There is a 36% chance the model is correct
questions.answer: 36% of the variance in the outcome is explained by the predictor(s)
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 36% of the variance in the outcome is explained by the predictor(s).

questions.question: A confounder in regression analysis is:
options: A variable with no effect on the outcome
A variable that is related to both the predictor and the outcome, potentially distorting results
The intercept of the regression line
An observation with a very high residual
questions.answer: A variable that is related to both the predictor and the outcome, potentially distorting results
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A variable that is related to both the predictor and the outcome, potentially distorting results.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A 95% confidence interval for a mean means:" is: There is a 95% probability the true mean is in this specific interval.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: If we repeated sampling many times, 95% of the intervals constructed would contain the true mean.

questions.question: As sample size increases, the margin of error in a confidence interval:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Decreases.

questions.question: A p-value of 0.04 means:
options: There is a 4% probability the null hypothesis is true
If the null hypothesis were true, there is a 4% chance of observing results this extreme or more
questions.answer: If the null hypothesis were true, there is a 4% chance of observing results this extreme or more
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: If the null hypothesis were true, there is a 4% chance of observing results this extreme or more.

questions.question: A chi-square test is used to:
options: Test relationships between two categorical variables
Calculate correlation between two continuous variables
questions.answer: Test relationships between two categorical variables
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Test relationships between two categorical variables.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The replication crisis in social science refers to:" is: The widespread failure to reproduce many published study findings.
questions.explanation: True. The widespread failure to reproduce many published study findings is correct.

questions.question: A graph that starts its y-axis at 70 instead of 0 for data ranging from 72% to 78% is an example of:
options: A misleading visualization that exaggerates differences
questions.answer: A misleading visualization that exaggerates differences
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A misleading visualization that exaggerates differences.

questions.question: Which study design best establishes causation?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A randomized controlled experiment.

questions.question: When ice cream sales and drowning rates are positively correlated, the most likely explanation is:
options: Drowning causes people to eat ice cream
A third variable (hot weather) causes both
The correlation is purely coincidental and meaningless
questions.answer: A third variable (hot weather) causes both
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: A third variable (hot weather) causes both.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Publication bias in social science means:" is: Journals are biased against certain topics.
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Studies with significant results are more likely to be published than null results.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best indicator that a data project is rigorous?
options: The conclusions confirm what the researcher expected
The sample is large, representative, and the analysis matches the research question
The report uses complex statistical jargon
The dataset comes from a government source
questions.answer: The sample is large, representative, and the analysis matches the research question
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: The sample is large, representative, and the analysis matches the research question.

questions.question: A researcher finds that countries with more TVs per capita have lower infant mortality rates. Explain why this correlation does not mean TVs reduce infant mortality. What concept explains this relationship, and what would a better study design look like?
questions.answer: TVs per capita is a proxy for overall wealth; wealthier countries both own more TVs and have better healthcare. The concept is confounding: a third variable (national wealth) causes both outcomes. A better design would control for GDP per capita or use a natural experiment comparing regions with and without TV access while holding economic conditions constant.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: TVs per capita is a proxy for overall wealth; wealthier countries both own more TVs and have better healthcare. The concept is confounding: a third variable (national wealth) causes both outcomes. A better design would control for GDP per capita or use a natural experiment comparing regions with and without TV access while holding economic conditions constant..

questions.question: You are hired to study whether a new after-school tutoring program improves test scores. Describe how you would design this study to produce valid, generalizable results. Identify the key statistical tools you would use from this course.
questions.answer: I would randomly assign students to a treatment group (tutoring) and a control group (no tutoring) to ensure causal inference. I would use a large, representative sample from the target population. Pre- and post-test scores would be compared using a t-test for independent samples to assess significance, a confidence interval to estimate the effect size range, and a regression controlling for prior achievement and socioeconomic status to isolate the program's effect. R² would indicate how much variance in scores the model explains.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: I would randomly assign students to a treatment group (tutoring) and a control group (no tutoring) to ensure causal inference. I would use a large, representative sample from the target population. Pre- and post-test scores would be compared using a t-test for independent samples to assess significance, a confidence interval to estimate the effect size range, and a regression controlling for prior achievement and socioeconomic status to isolate the program's effect. R² would indicate how much variance in scores the model explains..

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary purpose of statistics in social science?
options: To prove that researchers are right
To describe patterns and test claims about social phenomena systematically
To replace common sense with math
To collect more data than needed
quizQuestions.answer: To describe patterns and test claims about social phenomena systematically
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: To describe patterns and test claims about social phenomena systematically.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an example of statistics influencing public policy?
options: A journalist writing an opinion piece
A government using crime rate data to allocate police resources
quizQuestions.answer: A government using crime rate data to allocate police resources
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: A government using crime rate data to allocate police resources.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The primary purpose of statistics in social science is "To describe patterns and test claims about social phenomena systematically".
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. To describe patterns and test claims about social phenomena systematically is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Race (e.g., Asian, Black, White, Other) is an example of which level of measurement?
quizQuestions.answer: Nominal
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Nominal.

quizQuestions.question: Educational attainment coded as 1=Less than HS, 2=HS diploma, 3=Some college, 4=Bachelor's, 5=Graduate is:
quizQuestions.answer: Ordinal
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Ordinal.

quizQuestions.question: Temperature measured in Celsius is at which level of measurement?
quizQuestions.answer: Interval
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Interval.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Race (e.g., Asian, Black, White, Other) is an example of which level of measurement" is: Nominal.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Nominal is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Which sampling method gives every member of the population an equal chance of selection?
quizQuestions.answer: Simple random sampling
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Simple random sampling.

quizQuestions.question: Systematic sampling selects respondents by:
options: Choosing every nth person from a list
quizQuestions.answer: Choosing every nth person from a list
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Choosing every nth person from a list.

quizQuestions.question: A leading question in a survey is one that:
options: Nudges the respondent toward a particular answer
quizQuestions.answer: Nudges the respondent toward a particular answer
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Nudges the respondent toward a particular answer.

quizQuestions.question: A frequency table shows:
options: How many times each value or category occurs in the data
The predicted values from a regression
quizQuestions.answer: How many times each value or category occurs in the data
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: How many times each value or category occurs in the data.

quizQuestions.question: A histogram is best used to display:
options: Continuous or interval/ratio data grouped into bins
quizQuestions.answer: Continuous or interval/ratio data grouped into bins
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Continuous or interval/ratio data grouped into bins.

quizQuestions.question: Positive skewness in a distribution means:
options: The mean is less than the median
The tail extends to the right, with most data clustered on the left
quizQuestions.answer: The tail extends to the right, with most data clustered on the left
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The tail extends to the right, with most data clustered on the left.

quizQuestions.question: In a negatively skewed distribution of incomes, we would expect:
options: No relationship between mean and median
quizQuestions.answer: Mean < Median
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Mean < Median.

quizQuestions.question: In a dataset of {2, 3, 3, 4, 8}, the median is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 3.

quizQuestions.question: The mode of a dataset is:
options: The difference between max and min
quizQuestions.answer: The most frequently occurring value
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The most frequently occurring value.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "In a dataset of {2, 3, 3, 4, 8}, the median is:" is: 3.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. 3 is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The mode of a dataset is:" is: The average of all values.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The most frequently occurring value.

quizQuestions.question: The probability of flipping a fair coin and getting heads is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 0.5.

quizQuestions.question: Relative risk compares:
options: The probability of an outcome in one group to its probability in another group
The odds of two unrelated events
quizQuestions.answer: The probability of an outcome in one group to its probability in another group
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The probability of an outcome in one group to its probability in another group.

quizQuestions.question: An odds ratio of 1.0 indicates:
options: No difference in odds between the two groups
quizQuestions.answer: No difference in odds between the two groups
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: No difference in odds between the two groups.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The probability of flipping a fair coin and getting heads is:" is: 0.5.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. 0.5 is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The normal distribution is sometimes called the 'bell curve' because:
options: It was invented by Alexander Graham Bell
It is shaped symmetrically like a bell, with most data near the mean
Its area sums to the number of bells in a dataset
quizQuestions.answer: It is shaped symmetrically like a bell, with most data near the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: It is shaped symmetrically like a bell, with most data near the mean.

quizQuestions.question: A z-score converts a raw score by expressing it as:
options: The number of standard deviations above or below the mean
quizQuestions.answer: The number of standard deviations above or below the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The number of standard deviations above or below the mean.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The normal distribution is sometimes called the 'bell curve' because:" is: It is shaped symmetrically like a bell, with most data near the mean.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. It is shaped symmetrically like a bell, with most data near the mean is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A scatterplot displaying a pattern from lower-left to upper-right indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: A positive correlation
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: A positive correlation.

quizQuestions.question: Pearson r ranges from:
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: -1 to +1.

quizQuestions.question: A Pearson r of 0.05 indicates:
quizQuestions.answer: A weak or negligible relationship
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: A weak or negligible relationship.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A scatterplot displaying a pattern from lower-left to upper-right indicates:" is: A positive correlation.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A positive correlation is correct.

quizQuestions.question: In the regression equation ŷ = b₀ + b₁x, b₀ represents:
options: The predicted change in y per unit of x
The y-intercept — the predicted value of y when x = 0
quizQuestions.answer: The y-intercept — the predicted value of y when x = 0
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The y-intercept — the predicted value of y when x = 0.

quizQuestions.question: The slope in a simple linear regression tells us:
options: How scattered the data are around the line
The predicted change in y for each one-unit increase in x
The correlation between x and y squared
quizQuestions.answer: The predicted change in y for each one-unit increase in x
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The predicted change in y for each one-unit increase in x.

quizQuestions.question: R² = 0.64 means:
options: 64% of the variance in y is explained by the regression model
There is a 64% chance the slope is non-zero
quizQuestions.answer: 64% of the variance in y is explained by the regression model
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: 64% of the variance in y is explained by the regression model.

quizQuestions.question: Multiple regression differs from simple regression in that it:
options: Uses a different formula for the intercept
Includes two or more predictor variables simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: Includes two or more predictor variables simultaneously
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Includes two or more predictor variables simultaneously.

quizQuestions.question: In multiple regression, 'controlling for a variable' means:
options: Holding it constant statistically while examining other relationships
quizQuestions.answer: Holding it constant statistically while examining other relationships
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Holding it constant statistically while examining other relationships.

quizQuestions.question: A confounder is a variable that:
options: Has no relationship with the outcome
Is the main predictor of interest
Is related to both the predictor and the outcome, creating a spurious association
Increases R² without adding predictive value
quizQuestions.answer: Is related to both the predictor and the outcome, creating a spurious association
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Is related to both the predictor and the outcome, creating a spurious association.

quizQuestions.question: Adding predictors to a regression model generally:
options: Always improves prediction in new samples
Increases R² in the sample but may reduce generalizability if irrelevant predictors are added
Has no effect unless predictors are correlated
quizQuestions.answer: Increases R² in the sample but may reduce generalizability if irrelevant predictors are added
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Increases R² in the sample but may reduce generalizability if irrelevant predictors are added.

quizQuestions.question: A confidence interval provides:
options: A range of plausible values for the population parameter
The probability that the sample is representative
A single best-guess estimate with no uncertainty
quizQuestions.answer: A range of plausible values for the population parameter
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: A range of plausible values for the population parameter.

quizQuestions.question: The margin of error in a poll is directly related to:
options: The number of questions in the survey
The sample size — larger samples produce smaller margins of error
The response rate of the poll
quizQuestions.answer: The sample size — larger samples produce smaller margins of error
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: The sample size — larger samples produce smaller margins of error.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A confidence interval provides:" is: A range of plausible values for the population parameter.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. A range of plausible values for the population parameter is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The margin of error in a poll is directly related to:" is: The number of questions in the survey.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The sample size — larger samples produce smaller margins of error.

quizQuestions.question: The null hypothesis (H₀) in social research typically states:
options: That the researcher's prediction is correct
That there is no effect or no difference
quizQuestions.answer: That there is no effect or no difference
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: That there is no effect or no difference.

quizQuestions.question: A p-value of 0.001 compared to 0.04 indicates:
options: Weaker evidence against the null hypothesis
Stronger evidence against the null hypothesis
quizQuestions.answer: Stronger evidence against the null hypothesis
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Stronger evidence against the null hypothesis.

quizQuestions.question: A t-test is used to:
options: Test whether two group means are significantly different
quizQuestions.answer: Test whether two group means are significantly different
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Test whether two group means are significantly different.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The null hypothesis (H₀) in social research typically states:" is: That there is no effect or no difference.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. That there is no effect or no difference is correct.

quizQuestions.question: An observational study differs from an experiment because:
options: Researchers observe without manipulating variables, so causal inference is limited
It always has a control group
quizQuestions.answer: Researchers observe without manipulating variables, so causal inference is limited
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: Researchers observe without manipulating variables, so causal inference is limited.

quizQuestions.question: A graph that omits the zero baseline on the y-axis can be misleading because:
options: It changes the scale to ratio level
It makes small differences appear much larger than they are
quizQuestions.answer: It makes small differences appear much larger than they are
quizQuestions.explanation: The correct answer is: It makes small differences appear much larger than they are.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "An observational study differs from an experiment because:" is: Researchers observe without manipulating variables, so causal inference is limited.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Researchers observe without manipulating variables, so causal inference is limited is correct.
